content.text: WebSockets are a communication protocol that establishes a persistent, full-duplex channel over a single TCP connection between a client and a server. Unlike the traditional HTTP request-response cycle, where the client must initiate every exchange and the server can only reply, a WebSocket connection allows both parties to send messages to each other independently and at any time — more like a phone call than sending letters.
Defined in RFC 6455 and standardized in 2011, WebSockets are natively supported in all modern browsers via the
JavaScript API, and have mature server-side implementations in virtually every language. The protocol begins its life as an ordinary HTTP/1.1 request — the client sends an
header, and if the server agrees, the connection transitions from HTTP to the WebSocket protocol (opcode
). From that point forward, the connection is no longer HTTP; it operates using lightweight WebSocket frames that carry far less overhead than full HTTP headers.

In the context of agentic AI systems, WebSockets are the foundation for streaming LLM token output to users in real time, allowing AI agents to push intermediate results, tool invocation statuses, and plan updates back to a client as they happen rather than waiting for a complete response to be assembled server-side. They are the plumbing behind the word-by-word typing effect you see in chat interfaces like Claude.ai.

Before WebSockets, developers faced a painful dilemma for building real-time web applications. HTTP polling was the dominant workaround: the client would send a request every few seconds asking "anything new yet?" Most of the time the server replied "no" — burning bandwidth, server resources, and battery life for nothing. Alternatively, developers used long-polling (holding the HTTP connection open until data was available), which improved latency but consumed server threads while waiting.

Server-Sent Events (SSE) partially solved the server-to-client direction, allowing the server to push a stream of updates over a long-lived HTTP response. But SSE is unidirectional — clients cannot send messages back over the same connection without opening a separate HTTP request. This makes interactive or bidirectional real-time communication awkward.

WebSockets solve all of these problems in one move: one handshake opens a channel that both sides can write to freely, without the overhead of repeated HTTP headers, without polling delays, and without splitting bidirectional communication across two separate connections. For agentic AI applications in particular, this matters because:
LLMs generate tokens one by one. WebSockets let the server push each token to the UI immediately, delivering perceived sub-second latency instead of making the user wait for the complete response.
Agents running multi-step plans (tool calls, retrieval, reasoning) can push status updates — "calling search tool", "reading document", "drafting answer" — back to the user in real time.
Multiple clients or agents can participate in the same WebSocket channel, enabling shared workspaces, auction protocols, or orchestrated agent swarms that coordinate over a single persistent channel.

In autonomous agent systems that control external tools or interfaces, acknowledging that an action was received and executed needs to happen fast. WebSockets eliminate the round-trip overhead of a fresh HTTP connection.
Both client and server can send messages independently at any time. This is impossible in the standard HTTP model without workarounds.
After the handshake, WebSocket frames have a header of only 2–14 bytes compared to hundreds of bytes for HTTP headers. This is critical for high-frequency, small-payload messages like LLM tokens.
No connection setup per message. The TCP connection is already established, so messages can be delivered with network-only latency — typically a few milliseconds on a local network.
The WebSocket API is built into all modern browsers with no plugins or polyfills required, making it the natural choice for client-facing real-time features.
WebSocket frames carry arbitrary text or binary data. You can use JSON, MessagePack, Protocol Buffers, or any format you choose inside the frame.
WebSockets begin as HTTP and upgrade in-place, meaning they work through most existing load balancers, firewalls, and TLS termination points that support HTTP/1.1.

Every open WebSocket connection is a piece of server state. Scaling to 100,000 concurrent connections requires careful resource management, unlike stateless HTTP where any server can handle any request.

Standard HTTP load balancers can distribute requests round-robin across servers. WebSocket connections must be pinned to the same server for their lifetime (sticky sessions), or you need a shared pub-sub layer (like Redis) behind the scenes.

WebSocket frames have no native concept of "response to request X". If you need correlation (e.g., "here is the response to the message you sent with id=42"), you must implement that in your application protocol.
The WebSocket spec does not define automatic reconnection. Network drops, server restarts, or idle timeouts will close the connection and your client code must detect and re-establish it.

WebSockets were designed for HTTP/1.1. While RFC 8441 defines WebSockets over HTTP/2, support is inconsistent across infrastructure. HTTP/3 (QUIC) has its own competing streaming mechanism (HTTP/3 datagrams) that may eventually supersede WebSockets in some use cases.
WebSocket communication cannot be cached by CDNs or reverse proxies. Debug tools like curl cannot inspect WebSocket traffic trivially. This can complicate observability and testing.
A WebSocket connection starts as a plain HTTP GET request. The critical headers are
. The client also sends a randomly generated 16-byte base64-encoded nonce in the
The server validates the request, computes a response key by concatenating the client's key with the fixed GUID string
, hashing with SHA-1, and base64-encoding the result. This value is returned in the

header of the 101 Switching Protocols response. This handshake proves that the server genuinely understood the WebSocket upgrade request and is not a misconfigured HTTP server that echoed back arbitrary headers.
value and GUID is the fixed magic string from RFC 6455.
Once upgraded, communication uses WebSocket frames. Each frame has a small binary header followed by payload data. The structure is:
Indicates this is the final fragment of a message. Most single-frame messages set this to 1.
Reserved for extensions (e.g., per-message compression via the permessage-deflate extension uses RSV1).
Defines the frame type: 0x1 = text, 0x2 = binary, 0x8 = close, 0x9 = ping, 0xA = pong.
Frames from client to server must always be masked. Server-to-client frames must not be masked. Masking uses a 4-byte XOR key to prevent cache poisoning attacks on intermediaries.
If the 7-bit length field is 126, the next 2 bytes give the true length. If 127, the next 8 bytes give the length.

The minimum WebSocket frame header is just 2 bytes for a small unmasked server frame. Compare this to a typical HTTP response header of 300–800 bytes — the efficiency advantage is significant for high-frequency small messages like individual LLM tokens.
The overhead ratio for a single token message (say, 4 bytes of text) is:

To detect broken connections (e.g., a mobile client that lost WiFi but didn't send a TCP FIN), the WebSocket protocol defines ping and pong control frames. A server sends a ping; the client must respond with a pong containing the same payload data. If no pong arrives within a timeout window, the server can close the connection and free resources. This mechanism is essential for long-lived connections in production.

The following diagram shows the full lifecycle of a WebSocket connection between an AI agent client and a streaming inference server. The connection progresses through three distinct phases: HTTP upgrade negotiation, persistent bidirectional message exchange, and graceful closure.

attrs.alt: WebSocket connection lifecycle diagram showing HTTP upgrade handshake, bidirectional message frames, ping/pong heartbeat, and close frame

content.text: Notice that the TCP connection established in Phase 1 is reused for all subsequent message exchange in Phase 2. There is no per-message connection overhead. The server can push streaming token frames as fast as the LLM generates them, and the client can send control messages ("stop generating", "new prompt") over the same channel without opening a second connection.

Think of HTTP as sending physical mail: you write a letter (request), seal it, and post it. The recipient reads it, writes a reply, and posts it back. This works fine for most correspondence, but it is hopeless for a live phone conversation.

WebSockets are that phone call. Once you dial (the handshake), both parties have a live, open channel. Either side can say something at any moment. The call stays open until one party says goodbye. Every individual exchange does not need its own envelope, stamp, or postal journey — you just talk.

With this mental model, agentic AI use becomes intuitive: when an LLM is generating a long response, it is like someone thinking out loud. WebSockets let you hear each word as it is formed, not wait in silence until they finish their entire speech and hand you a transcription.
When to Use It — and When Not To
Streaming server output to the client:
LLM token streaming, live log tailing, real-time analytics dashboards, sports scores.
Chat applications, collaborative document editing (like Google Docs), multiplayer games, shared whiteboards.
The server needs to push notifications or updates to the client without waiting for a client poll — stock price updates, sensor readings, agent status updates.
When you expect more than a few messages per second, WebSocket frame overhead is far more efficient than repeated HTTP connections or even HTTP/2 streams.
When an AI agent and its client maintain a shared conversation or task context over minutes or hours, a persistent WebSocket connection models that statefulness naturally.
Fetching a user profile, submitting a form, loading a page — standard HTTP is simpler, cacheable, and well understood. WebSockets add complexity without benefit.
If the client never needs to send messages after the initial request, Server-Sent Events (SSE) is simpler, works over plain HTTP/2, is automatically reconnecting, and is supported by every CDN.
Polling once per minute is far simpler than maintaining a persistent connection. The connection overhead of polling at low frequency is negligible.
Some corporate or cloud proxy configurations block or timeout WebSocket upgrades. In these environments SSE or long-polling may be more reliable.

# For Python async server/client
pip install websockets

# For a FastAPI application (built on Starlette)
pip install fastapi uvicorn websockets

# Node.js (server)
npm install ws
Step 2: Write a minimal Python WebSocket server

# server.py — minimal echo server using the 'websockets' library
import asyncio
import websockets
import json

CLIENTS: set = set()

async def handler(websocket):
    """Handle one client connection for its lifetime."""
    CLIENTS.add(websocket)
    try:
        async for message in websocket:
            data = json.loads(message)
            print(f"Received: {data}")
            # Echo back with a timestamp field added
            response = {**data, "echo": True}
            await websocket.send(json.dumps(response))
    except websockets.exceptions.ConnectionClosedOK:
        pass  # Client disconnected gracefully
    finally:
        CLIENTS.discard(websocket)

async def main():
    # Serve on all interfaces, port 8765
    async with websockets.serve(handler, "0.0.0.0", 8765):
        print("WebSocket server listening on ws://localhost:8765")
        await asyncio.Future()  # Run forever

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
Step 3: Write a Python WebSocket client

# client.py — connect, send a message, receive the echo
import asyncio
import websockets
import json

async def main():
    uri = "ws://localhost:8765"
    async with websockets.connect(uri) as ws:
        # Send a message
        msg = {"type": "greet", "text": "Hello, server!"}
        await ws.send(json.dumps(msg))
        print(f"Sent: {msg}")

        # Receive the echo
        reply = json.loads(await ws.recv())
        print(f"Received: {reply}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
Step 4: Rebuild app data and verify
After implementing the server and client, test the full round-trip by running the server in one terminal (
). You should see the sent message echoed back with the added
Example 1: LLM Token Streaming via WebSocket
This example shows a FastAPI server that streams Anthropic Claude tokens to a WebSocket client in real time. Each token is pushed immediately rather than buffered until the response is complete.

# streaming_server.py — FastAPI WebSocket + Anthropic streaming
import asyncio
import json
import anthropic
from fastapi import FastAPI, WebSocket, WebSocketDisconnect

app = FastAPI()
client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env

@app.websocket("/ws/chat")
async def websocket_chat(websocket: WebSocket):
    await websocket.accept()
    try:
        while True:
            # Wait for the client to send a prompt
            raw = await websocket.receive_text()
            payload = json.loads(raw)
            user_prompt = payload.get("prompt", "")

            if not user_prompt:
                await websocket.send_text(json.dumps({"type": "error", "message": "No prompt provided"}))
                continue

            # Stream from Claude using the streaming context manager
            with client.messages.stream(
                model="claude-opus-4-5",
                max_tokens=1024,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_prompt}],
            ) as stream:
                # Push each text delta to the client as it arrives
                for text in stream.text_stream:
                    await websocket.send_text(json.dumps({
                        "type": "token",
                        "content": text,
                    }))

            # Signal that the response is complete
            await websocket.send_text(json.dumps({"type": "done"}))

    except WebSocketDisconnect:
        print("Client disconnected")

# Run with: uvicorn streaming_server:app --reload
Example 2: Browser Client Receiving Streamed Tokens

# ws_stream_client.py — Python client that renders streamed tokens to stdout
import asyncio
import json
import sys
import websockets

async def chat(prompt: str):
    uri = "ws://localhost:8000/ws/chat"
    async with websockets.connect(uri) as ws:
        # Send the prompt
        await ws.send(json.dumps({"prompt": prompt}))
        print("Assistant: ", end="", flush=True)

        # Read frames until the server signals done
        async for message in ws:
            data = json.loads(message)
            if data["type"] == "token":
                print(data["content"], end="", flush=True)
            elif data["type"] == "done":
                print()  # Newline after response
                break
            elif data["type"] == "error":
                print(f"\nError: {data['message']}")
                break

if __name__ == "__main__":
    prompt = " ".join(sys.argv[1:]) or "Explain WebSockets in one paragraph."
    asyncio.run(chat(prompt))

# broadcast_server.py — push a message to ALL connected clients
import asyncio
import json
import websockets
from datetime import datetime, timezone

CLIENTS: set[websockets.ServerConnection] = set()

async def handler(websocket):
    CLIENTS.add(websocket)
    try:
        async for message in websocket:
            data = json.loads(message)
            # Broadcast to every other connected client
            broadcast_msg = json.dumps({
                "from": data.get("username", "anonymous"),
                "text": data.get("text", ""),
                "at": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
            })
            # Send to all clients concurrently (including sender for echo)
            if CLIENTS:
                await asyncio.gather(
                    *[c.send(broadcast_msg) for c in CLIENTS],
                    return_exceptions=True,
                )
    except websockets.exceptions.ConnectionClosedOK:
        pass
    finally:
        CLIENTS.discard(websocket)

async def main():
    async with websockets.serve(handler, "0.0.0.0", 8765):
        await asyncio.Future()

asyncio.run(main())
Real-World Example — Production AI Chat Backend

Consider a production AI assistant application serving 5,000 concurrent users. Each user has an open WebSocket connection to an application server that orchestrates calls to an LLM provider. The key constraints are:
5,000 concurrent open TCP connections per server instance (manageable with async I/O; each connection consumes ~8 KB of kernel buffer)
Average session duration: 8 minutes, with token streaming for 30–60 seconds of each session
Median tokens per response: 400 tokens, average token latency from provider: 15 ms/token, so each stream lasts about 6 seconds
Must support client-side "stop generating" without killing the HTTP request — WebSocket makes this trivial (client sends a stop frame; server cancels the stream context)

# production_agent_ws.py — production-grade pattern with connection management
import asyncio
import json
import logging
import uuid
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

import anthropic
import websockets
from websockets.server import WebSocketServerProtocol

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()  # async client for concurrent sessions

@dataclass
class Session:
    session_id: str
    websocket: WebSocketServerProtocol
    messages: list = field(default_factory=list)
    current_task: Optional[asyncio.Task] = None

    def add_user_message(self, content: str):
        self.messages.append({"role": "user", "content": content})

    def add_assistant_message(self, content: str):
        self.messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": content})

async def stream_response(session: Session, prompt: str):
    """Stream a Claude response, appending to conversation history."""
    session.add_user_message(prompt)
    full_response = []
    try:
        async with client.messages.stream(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=2048,
            messages=session.messages,
        ) as stream:
            async for text in stream.text_stream:
                full_response.append(text)
                await session.websocket.send(json.dumps({
                    "type": "token",
                    "session_id": session.session_id,
                    "content": text,
                }))
        # Persist complete assistant turn in history
        session.add_assistant_message("".join(full_response))
        await session.websocket.send(json.dumps({
            "type": "done",
            "session_id": session.session_id,
            "total_tokens": len(full_response),
        }))
    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        # Client sent stop signal — gracefully truncate
        if full_response:
            session.add_assistant_message("".join(full_response) + " [stopped]")
        await session.websocket.send(json.dumps({
            "type": "stopped",
            "session_id": session.session_id,
        }))

async def handler(websocket: WebSocketServerProtocol):
    session = Session(
        session_id=str(uuid.uuid4()),
        websocket=websocket,
    )
    # Announce session ID to client
    await websocket.send(json.dumps({
        "type": "session_start",
        "session_id": session.session_id,
    }))
    logger.info(f"Session started: {session.session_id}")

    try:
        async for raw in websocket:
            data = json.loads(raw)
            msg_type = data.get("type")

            if msg_type == "prompt":
                # Cancel any in-progress stream before starting a new one
                if session.current_task and not session.current_task.done():
                    session.current_task.cancel()
                    await asyncio.sleep(0)  # Yield to let cancel propagate
                session.current_task = asyncio.create_task(
                    stream_response(session, data["content"])
                )

            elif msg_type == "stop":
                if session.current_task and not session.current_task.done():
                    session.current_task.cancel()

    except websockets.exceptions.ConnectionClosedOK:
        logger.info(f"Session ended cleanly: {session.session_id}")
    except websockets.exceptions.ConnectionClosedError as e:
        logger.warning(f"Session {session.session_id} closed with error: {e}")
    finally:
        if session.current_task and not session.current_task.done():
            session.current_task.cancel()

async def main():
    async with websockets.serve(
        handler,
        "0.0.0.0",
        8765,
        ping_interval=20,   # Send ping every 20 seconds
        ping_timeout=10,    # Close if no pong within 10 seconds
        max_size=1_048_576, # 1 MB max message size
    ):
        logger.info("Production WebSocket server running on ws://0.0.0.0:8765")
        await asyncio.Future()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
    asyncio.run(main())
Mistake 1: Forgetting that client frames must be masked

The WebSocket spec mandates that all frames from client to server must be masked. Most client libraries do this automatically. The mistake is trying to hand-craft raw WebSocket frames or using a non-compliant library — the server will reject unmasked client frames with a 1002 Protocol Error close code.

WebSocket connections drop. Mobile networks switch between WiFi and cellular. Servers restart for deployments. If your client code does not implement reconnection logic, users get a silent dead connection.

# WRONG: fires and forgets the connection
async with websockets.connect("ws://localhost:8765") as ws:
    await ws.send("hello")
    response = await ws.recv()  # If connection drops here, exception propagates and dies

# CORRECT: reconnect with exponential backoff
import asyncio
import websockets

async def listen_with_reconnect(uri: str):
    backoff = 1
    while True:
        try:
            async with websockets.connect(uri) as ws:
                backoff = 1  # Reset on successful connect
                print(f"Connected to {uri}")
                async for message in ws:
                    process(message)
        except (websockets.exceptions.ConnectionClosed, OSError) as e:
            print(f"Connection lost ({e}), retrying in {backoff}s...")
            await asyncio.sleep(backoff)
            backoff = min(backoff * 2, 60)  # Cap at 60 seconds

def process(message):
    print(f"Got: {message}")
Mistake 3: Using WebSocket for simple request-response

WebSockets add significant operational complexity compared to HTTP. If a feature does not require streaming, push notifications, or bidirectional real-time communication, a plain HTTP endpoint is simpler, easier to test, easier to cache, and easier to debug. Default to HTTP and upgrade to WebSockets only when you hit HTTP's limits.
If the server sends messages faster than the client can process them, the WebSocket send buffer fills up. Unchecked, this causes memory exhaustion on the server. Always
your send calls (do not fire-and-forget with
) and monitor send queue depth in production.

Abruptly dropping a TCP connection (e.g., killing the server process) leaves the client in an ambiguous state. Always send a close frame with an appropriate close code before shutting down, and handle the peer's close frame by completing the close handshake:

# Graceful server shutdown — send close frame before exiting
async def shutdown(websocket):
    await websocket.close(code=1001, reason="Server shutting down")
    # 1001 = Going Away (server or browser is going away)
Unencrypted ws:// exposes all WebSocket traffic to network observers. TLS (wss://) also prevents some corporate proxies from interfering with the connection.

WebSocket frames are opaque bytes. Define a clear JSON schema (or use a sub-protocol via the Sec-WebSocket-Protocol header) for your messages: a 'type' field, versioning, and a 'id' field for request-response correlation.

Use library-level ping_interval and ping_timeout settings (e.g., ping_interval=20, ping_timeout=10 in the 'websockets' library). Do not rely on TCP keepalives alone — they operate at a longer timescale.
Implement reconnection with exponential backoff on the client:
Add jitter to avoid thundering-herd reconnection storms when a server restarts and thousands of clients reconnect simultaneously.
Reject messages above a reasonable threshold (1 MB is common) to prevent memory exhaustion from malformed or malicious clients.
Log session open, close, and close code at INFO level. Log unexpected errors at WARNING/ERROR. This is your primary observability tool for WebSocket sessions.
Keep WebSocket endpoints (/ws/...) separate from REST API paths (/api/...). This makes routing, authentication middleware, and load balancer configuration cleaner.
Pass a JWT or session token in the initial request (query parameter or first message after connect). Do not rely on cookies alone — WebSocket subprotocol handshake handling varies.

A single asyncio-based Python WebSocket server can handle tens of thousands of concurrent idle connections on modern hardware. The key insight is that Python's async I/O model multiplexes all connections on a small thread pool; the limiting resource is memory (each connection carries OS socket buffers and Python object overhead) rather than CPU.
At 12 KB per connection, a server with 4 GB of available RAM can theoretically hold
simultaneous WebSocket connections. In practice, you hit CPU limits from message processing at around 50,000–100,000 concurrent active (not just connected) sessions per core.
WebSocket connections are inherently sticky — a client connected to server A cannot receive messages published to server B without a shared messaging layer. The standard solution is a pub-sub broker:
Each WebSocket server subscribes to a Redis pub-sub channel (or uses a message queue like RabbitMQ/Kafka).
When a message must be broadcast or routed to a specific client, any server publishes to Redis.
The server that holds that client's WebSocket connection forwards the message to the client.
This decouples horizontal scaling of WebSocket servers from message delivery logic.

Note that LLM token throughput is generally bounded by the inference provider, not by WebSocket infrastructure. Optimizing for raw WebSocket throughput is rarely the bottleneck in AI agent applications.
The canonical Python async WebSocket library. Clean asyncio-native API, actively maintained, excellent documentation. Best for standalone WebSocket servers and clients.

FastAPI wraps Starlette's WebSocket support. Use this when you need both HTTP REST endpoints and WebSocket endpoints in the same application. The WebSocket object is injected via dependency injection.
The most popular WebSocket library for Node.js. Minimal, fast, and well-maintained. Socket.IO (which runs on top of ws) adds rooms, namespaces, and automatic fallback to polling.
A command-line WebSocket client. Install with npm install -g wscat, then connect interactively: wscat -c ws://localhost:8765. Invaluable for manual testing.
Both tools now support WebSocket connections for GUI-based testing. You can send and receive messages visually without writing client code.
A fast, feature-rich command-line WebSocket client/server written in Rust. Supports piping stdin/stdout into WebSocket connections — useful for scripting.
The standard backend for WebSocket scaling. Use with the aioredis or redis-py library to publish messages that all WebSocket server instances relay to their connected clients.

For most AI streaming use cases (token streaming, agent status updates), Server-Sent Events are worth evaluating before reaching for WebSockets. SSE is simpler, works through HTTP/2 multiplexing, and includes built-in reconnection. The decisive factor is bidirectionality: if the client must send messages after the initial request (stop signals, follow-up prompts, configuration changes), WebSockets are the correct tool.

Unlike regular cookies, the WebSocket handshake is triggered by JavaScript and does include cookies in the HTTP upgrade request. A malicious third-party site can open a WebSocket connection to your server using the victim's session cookies, bypassing the same-origin policy. Mitigate by:
On the server, check that the Origin header in the upgrade request matches your expected domain. Reject connections from unknown origins.
Include a CSRF token in the WebSocket URL query parameter or the first message, and validate it server-side before processing any further messages.
Denial of Service via Connection Exhaustion
An attacker can open thousands of WebSocket connections, exhausting server file descriptors and memory. Mitigate by:
Rate-limiting WebSocket upgrade requests per IP at the load balancer level
Setting a maximum number of connections per authenticated user
Requiring authentication before accepting the upgrade (reject unauthenticated upgrade requests before the full WebSocket handshake completes)
Never trust WebSocket message content blindly. Validate message structure and bounds before processing:
(WebSocket Secure) in production. This is WebSocket traffic tunneled through TLS — the same security you get with HTTPS.

sends all message content in plaintext, including authentication tokens and user data. Most modern browsers will block mixed-content WebSocket connections (a page served over HTTPS attempting to open a ws:// connection).
Configure server-side ping intervals and timeouts. Dead connections that do not respond to pings should be closed promptly to reclaim resources.
For critical message delivery, include a sequence number in each message. Clients can detect gaps in the sequence after reconnection and request replay.
Design server message handlers so that processing the same message twice has no additional effect. This allows safe replay on reconnection.
One HTTP upgrade establishes a persistent channel where both client and server can send messages at any time, eliminating the polling overhead and half-duplex limitations of standard HTTP.
The frame format is extremely lightweight:
2–14 bytes of header per message versus hundreds of bytes for HTTP headers — this efficiency advantage is decisive for high-frequency messages like LLM tokens.
WebSocket connections begin as HTTP/1.1 requests. This means they work through existing TLS termination, load balancers, and most firewalls without special configuration.
Stateful connections require different scaling strategies:
You cannot route WebSocket requests round-robin across servers without a shared pub-sub layer (typically Redis). Plan for sticky sessions or a message broker from the start.
The WebSocket spec does not define automatic reconnection. Every client must implement exponential backoff reconnection or users will silently lose their real-time connection.
Prefer SSE for unidirectional server push:
If the client only needs to receive data from the server (one-way token streaming with no mid-stream controls), Server-Sent Events are simpler, HTTP/2-native, and include built-in reconnection.
Security requires Origin validation and authentication:
Validate the Origin header on upgrade to prevent cross-site hijacking, enforce authentication before accepting the upgrade, and validate all message payloads with strict schemas.
For AI agents, WebSockets enable the streaming UX users expect:

The word-by-word typing effect in LLM chat interfaces, real-time agent status updates, and stop-generation controls all depend on a persistent WebSocket channel between the AI backend and the user interface.
